rows: Map power bases
Need to identify which stakeholders hold reward, coercive, referent, expert, or legitimate power.
Map resource dependencies
Salancik-Pfeffer mapping needed: who controls what critical resources?
Identify counterpower
Need to anticipate stakeholder push-back from independent power sources.
Apply the eight-question stakeholder analysis
Stakeholder mapping needs structure: who are they, what are their stakes, gain/lose, types of power, retention/win-over strategies.
Surface stakeholders without voice via TBL framing
The decision affects environment, future generations, communities, voiceless beneficiaries; people-planet-profit framing surfaces them explicitly.
Reject pure shareholder framing
Stakeholder list defaults to shareholders or financial-only stakeholders; the text rejects this as a primary framing.
Identify the six financial-reporting stakeholder categories
A disclosure or report has external audience; stockholders, creditors, regulators, customers, employees, and communities each have distinct information needs.
Map by information need to specific financial statements

Different stakeholders read different parts of the financial statements (analysts focus on income; lenders focus on debt and ratios; regulators focus on disclosure compliance; employees focus on going-concern).
Identify the audit committee's structural role
A judgement call needs board-level oversight; the audit committee, not management, oversees external-auditor work.
Identify externally-affected parties
Decision affects third parties not at the table; pollution downstream, learning effects on neighbours, public-health externalities of vaccination.
Map labour-market structure
Workforce engagement; need to identify whether the structural shape is competitive, monopsonistic, unionised, or bilateral monopoly.
Identify the kind of good at issue
Engagement around shared resource; matter at heart of the engagement is private, public, common-pool, or club good — institutional response varies.
Map information asymmetries
Lemons-problem dynamics suspected; need to identify who knows what others don't and what signalling devices reduce asymmetry.
Identify voiceless stakeholders
Future generations, environment, distant communities are affected but lack a formal seat; proxy representation needs design.
Apply Donaldson-Preston stakeholder-salience framing
Stakeholder claims are competing; the three-approach framework (descriptive, instrumental, normative) clarifies which perspective the engagement is operating from.
Map stakeholder moral standing explicitly
It is unclear which stakeholders have moral standing (not just economic or legal standing); the moral-minimum and social-contract framing resolves this.
Map the B2B buying centre
A commercial engagement has multiple decision roles (initiator, influencer, gatekeeper, buyer, decider, user) that need to be mapped before engagement design.
Map the consumer journey touchpoints
A customer-engagement effort needs to be timed to the consumer decision process (need recognition, information search, evaluation, purchase, post-purchase).
Identify the financial stakeholder categories
A capital or financing decision has equity holders, debt holders, and regulators with distinct rights and information needs; each needs to be mapped before engagement.
Identify legal standing in a dispute
A dispute or contract negotiation is forming; who has legal standing (privity of contract, tortfeasor, beneficiary, third-party rights) needs mapping before engagement.
Surface attachment and social-support network
An engagement involves individuals under stress or in transition; mapping their social-support network (emotional, instrumental, informational, appraisal) informs engagement design.
Identify stakeholders algorithmically from network data
The stakeholder set is large or unclear; PageRank-on-network gives a defensible quantitative ranking when the network is well-mapped (Lim's RALIC method).
Map information-asymmetry structure in vendor-client engagement
The relationship is vendor-client and one party (likely the vendor) has accumulated intimate familiarity that deters competing alternatives; identify the asymmetries that need contract mitigation.
Identify the full software-project stakeholder set

A software project is starting; surface clients, managers, users, governments, developers of integrated software, the development team — and check whether voices from underrepresented cognitive styles are missing.
Map team stakeholders by Tuckman stage
The team itself is a stakeholder set; where is it in the five-stage development arc (Forming, Storming, Norming, Performing, Adjourning)?
Assign decision rights via RACI
Role ambiguity is blocking engagement; explicitly assign Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed per task or deliverable.
Map the four stakeholder audiences in a patient-safety incident
Incident response involves managers, the affected staff member, patients, and families; each requires a different conversation but anchored to the same procedural artefact.
Identify the Scrum stakeholder layers

A Scrum (or analogously complex-work) project needs stakeholder mapping; surface customers/users, organisational leaders, regulators, the team itself, and any voiceless stakeholders the Product Owner must represent.
Surface "those wanting to change the Product Backlog"
Influence on the team's work is happening informally; surface who is engaging and route them through the Product Owner.
Identify Sprint Review attendees
Sprint Review attendance is uneven or unclear; who are the "key stakeholders" who need to be in the working session this Sprint?
Identify organisational barriers for Scrum Master to address
What organisational barriers between stakeholders and Scrum Team need the Scrum Master's attention? (Removing these is a named Scrum Master responsibility.)
Map the FLA stakeholder set after an unintended outcome

An unintended outcome has occurred; map the stakeholder set — those directly involved, supervisors, project leaders, Delegating Officials, families, cooperators, union, interagency partners, regulators, the community of practice.
Assign trust-bridge liaisons
Multiple liaison roles bridge the agency to stakeholder groups: Family, Hospital, CISM, CAP, EAP, agency representative, IMT, regional/state office, cooperator. Assign before the FLA team arrives.
Identify the silent voice in the stakeholder set
Quiet participants, junior crewmembers, contractors without permanent status, family members who lost someone — the silent voice needs deliberate engagement design before the group dialogue.
Map community-of-practice focus-group composition (Learning Review)
The Learning Review will produce recommendations; identify the relevant communities of practice (line officers, engine crewmembers, hotshots, helitack, sawyers, etc.) for focus-group vetting.
Identify roles whose voices must be present at any scale
The TC names FIST chief, RTO, friendly and OPFOR commanders, unit leaders as required at company-and-above scale. Identify our equivalent — the roles whose absence would compromise truth-finding.
Map the OPFOR-equivalent stakeholder

The party whose plan and intent shaped the action's outcome from the other side — competitor, counterparty, customer at a service failure, regulator at a compliance event. Include them in the engagement.
Identify the two-echelons-above independent evaluator
When independent assessment matters, immediate next echelon is too close. Map the two-up rule for the engagement.
Match attendance scale to engagement scale
Squad/platoon-equivalent: everyone attends; company-and-above-equivalent: key players only. Decide which we are before designing attendance.
Apply the per-event participant tier model
A Scrum event invitation list is being made ad hoc. The Field Guide names Required / Strongly Suggested / Invited tiers per event; start there.
Curate the Sprint Review invitation list deliberately
"Key stakeholders and end users invited by the Product Owner." End users are named explicitly. The PO curates; it's not an open invitation.
Name SMEs by domain need at Backlog Refinement
SMEs/stakeholders surface what the team can't — novel approaches, edge cases, risks. Even non-contributing developers benefit by learning the domain.
Identify distributed-engagement structural gaps
Distributed members lose informal interaction. The Daily Scrum bridge kept open for Parking-Lot topics is the structural replacement.
Apply the "Unusual Suspects" question
"Who's not here that could contribute to our conversation? This may be one of the most important questions you can ask." Engagement design starts upstream of the room.
Apply "Nothing About Me Without Me" as a design test
Reframe complaints-about-others as invitations: "Who could we talk with in [the absent group] that might be able to help us fix this?"
Map the stakeholder network
Network Weaving: participants draw maps; analytic moves include closing triangles, integrating clusters, and expanding the periphery with explicit demographic-representation prompts.
Identify historically-tokenised participants

"Many people have been to too many meetings where they were invited as window dressing without the opportunity to contribute, so they may be suspicious." Re-establish trust about how participation will matter.
Identify the team stakeholders
Open Kanban does not require role redefinition; the existing team membership is the engagement frame — developers, designers, analysts, testers, leads.
Identify cross-functional stakeholders
Who else needs visibility into the team's flow? Managers, downstream operations, customer support, dependent teams — they engage via the Kanban board or other information radiator.
Surface "person in authority" engagement
Open Kanban's Courage value names this stakeholder type — courage is the named lever for upward engagement when authority makes a mistake.
Identify field-level stakeholders
The Open Kanban Movement framing names fellow practitioners and the broader community as a layer of engagement (named: Shalloway, Ladas, Scotland).
Test the people-vs-resource framing
Are stakeholders being treated as inputs to a system or as constitutive parts of it? The Holistic / Systemic value puts people inside the system.
Map the participant population and readiness-for-stretch distribution
When characterising the cohort — prior experience, development stage, varied readiness for stretch (which feeds the track-architecture decision).
Identify co-facilitator candidates on complementarity dimensions
When picking co-facilitator — complementarity in skill mix, working hours, life rhythm.
Selecting stakeholders for a system-mapping engagement

The map of a complex system requires structural coverage; selection should triangulate "affect/affected", "on-the-ground/strategic-overview", "often overlooked", and "provocateurs". Diversity has a cost in process complexity but a benefit in system representation.
Designing a stakeholder-mapping workshop end-to-end
A workshop is being designed; the checklist covers planning, venue/materials/technology, facilitation, capturing/recording, post-workshop, common issues, and online-workshop adaptations.
Defending the use of stakeholder workshops as legitimate evidence

A stakeholder, client, or peer reviewer pushes back on "validation" of a participatory map; the defence rests on the unavailability of quantitative validation data for genuine complex adaptive systems, plus the value of maps as boundary objects.
Designing online stakeholder engagements that work
An engagement must run virtually; the design needs shorter sessions, more facilitators, tech-icebreaker, careful software choice, more follow-up one-to-ones.
Designing engagement to deliver process value rather than just product

The expected value of the engagement is in stakeholder learning, shared understanding, and ownership rather than (or in addition to) the finished map; the engagement design should be longer, more participatory, and more iterative.
Brainstorm stakeholder list
Starting a new engagement; risk of missing stakeholders
Name primary stakeholder concretely
Tendency to use generic "customer" or "user"
Map by power and interest
Need a communication plan calibrated to influence
Assign engagement roles
Confusion about who decides, who is consulted, who informed
Assume conflicting goals as the baseline

Multiple stakeholders with different goals is a *structural feature* of complex problems, not a process failure. Engagement design that designs for alignment-by-default produces lukewarm outcomes when alignment isn't there.
Map vantage-points, not roles
Each stakeholder's mental model is *partial-but-valid* because of position. Map participants by what part of the system they're experiencing, not by org-chart title.
Account for accumulated trust history

Stakeholders arrive carrying years of accumulated experience with the organisation, the topic, prior engagements. The "blank slate" failure mode is treating this first interaction as if it has no history.
Identify all affected archetype groups before mapping an org-change stakeholder set

An org-change initiative touches multiple OT archetypes; each archetype's people (Doing vs Directing, CAPS vs WHOLE vs TASKS) is a distinct stakeholder group with different engagement needs and different current voice.
Identify policy owners adjacent to the change (HR, Finance, reward)
Multi-level engagement is required; the HR and Finance policy owners whose structures will need to change in concert must be mapped before the engagement design, not discovered mid-rollout.
Test whether "people-as-resources" framing is suppressing voice in the stakeholder set

Resource Topology's "skill demand matched with skill supply" framing is named as low-ownership and low-voice. Map which groups are currently framed this way and what engagement design shift that demands.
Identify type of interdependence
Need to determine whether stakeholders are pooled, sequentially, or reciprocally interdependent.
Choose conflict-resolution mode
Need to decide between competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, or accommodating.
Choose bargaining frame
Need to determine whether the engagement is distributive (zero-sum) or integrative (positive-sum).
Choose ethical lens for engagement
The engagement question is fundamentally ethical; utilitarianism, universalism, rights, justice, virtue ethics, common good, relativism produce different strategies.
Apply Rest's moral-sensitivity check
The team has not yet recognised the moral component of the engagement; making it explicit shapes the convening and substance.
Apply news-test for engagement design
Uncertainty about whether the planned engagement crosses an ethical line; "front page tomorrow" test surfaces the answer.
Apply the full-disclosure principle
A material business activity could affect the financial statements; note disclosure or MD&A is the appropriate vehicle.
Apply the conservatism bias to disclosure framing
Uncertainty exists in an estimate; disclosure should not overstate value or understate liability.
Distinguish efficiency from distribution
Conflating "is this good in aggregate" with "who wins and loses" produces argument at cross purposes.
Surface elasticity-driven asymmetry
Whose options are elastic and whose are inelastic? Elastic side can walk away; inelastic side faces price-and-condition pressure.
Frame BATNA with structural awareness
In bilateral-monopoly settings, the wage / price / quantity range is set by relative bargaining position, not by an external benchmark.
Test for free-rider risk
Engagement design assumes voluntary contribution; nonexcludable benefits make this predictably under-fund.
Acknowledge regulatory-capture risk
Regulator is part of the engagement; whose interests dominate the rule-making process?
Apply the moral-minimum test
The engagement is being framed as a business decision; the moral-minimum question (what is the least we must do?) needs to be separated from the ethical-maximum question (what should we do?).
Choose between CSR framing layers
The engagement involves a corporate-responsibility question; the four-component pyramid (economic, legal, ethical, philanthropic) frames what is required vs what is aspirational.
Frame the market-communication strategy
The engagement involves communicating a value proposition to customers or external stakeholders; the ADAMS promotional mix and IMC framing selects the communication mode.
Frame the investor-communication question
A capital-markets engagement is being designed; the framing needs to address the risk-return question from the shareholder's perspective (CAPM, DDM, required return).
Frame the incident response around circumstances, not outcome
Stakeholder conversation about an incident is starting; the differentiation principle — response differs by circumstances, not by outcome — sets the frame for every audience.
Frame the staff conversation around procedural fairness
The conversation with the affected staff member needs a procedural anchor that protects from unfair targeting and preserves the wider-investigation parallel.
Frame the Sprint Review purpose
The Sprint Review is "to inspect the outcome of the Sprint and determine future adaptations" — a working session, not a status report or sign-off ceremony.
Frame the Sprint Retrospective purpose
The Sprint Retrospective is to plan ways to increase quality and effectiveness — covering individuals, interactions, processes, tools, and Definition of Done.
Set Sprint Planning advisor invitations
The Scrum Team may invite advisors to Sprint Planning; would a customer rep, domain expert, or operations lead strengthen the upcoming planning?
Anchor stakeholder conversation on the Product Goal
Stakeholder conversations lack a shared anchor; the Product Goal is the long-term objective the Product Backlog emerges to fulfill.
Frame the engagement around Just Culture

The stakeholder engagement is about an unintended outcome; frame around trust, fairness, dignity, and the administrative-firewall commitment. Without these conditions, honest accounts will not surface.
Lock the administrative firewall before engaging

The delegation of authority must explicitly commit to no disciplinary action, no forced retraining, no removal from incident, no reduction in planned overtime. This commitment frames every subsequent stakeholder conversation.
Frame accountability as account-giving, not punishment
Stakeholders default to the criminal-justice meaning of accountability; reframe as "an account is something you tell and learn from." The slang antonym *Accountabilism* names what to avoid.
Set the stage by explaining the FLA history and underpinnings
Stakeholders are anxious that "investigators" are out to get them; explain the history of FLA, the degree of confidentiality, Just Culture, and the Gap before substantive engagement begins.
Choose AAR mode vs critique mode
The choice shapes everything downstream. Critique mode delivers verdicts and produces self-defence; AAR mode surfaces what happened and why, and produces candour.
Identify the standard against which performance is being evaluated
Without a published criterion (the *Army-standard equivalent*), the engagement will drift toward opinion.
Separate grading from learning when both are required
An engagement that grades and learns in the same room produces less candour than two separate engagements. Plan accordingly.
Apply the non-interference rule explicitly
Have non-Team participants understood that "this is not their event and they may not interfere with the team's process, only support the team"? If not clear at the start, the engagement derails.
Pick the retrospective confidentiality rule
Vegas Rule (unanimous explicit consent before sharing) or Chatham House Rule (information may be shared, identities may not). Pick one explicitly — it's a precondition for difficult conversations.
Reframe Sprint Review as collaboration, not status
The Sprint Review is being run as a status presentation. Reframe up front: working session for collaboratively improving the Product Backlog.
Honour the Retrospective Prime Directive
Read or recall Norm Kerth's directive: "everyone did the best job they could, given what they knew at the time…" A retro without this framing tilts toward blame.
Don't show undone work
At the Sprint Review, demonstrate only Done items. Showing undone work erodes trust if it doesn't ship as expected and persists the illusion that incomplete work is valuable.
Test for "buy-in" framing in the engagement plan
If the goal is producing buy-in, that's a danger signal of insufficient up-front involvement.
Test for "expert-built scenarios for community buy-in" pattern
"Lack of ownership on the part of the whole community means that the scenarios don't really play a part in decision making." Re-frame as Participatory Scenarios.
Storyboard the engagement before designing the agenda
Diagnostic: "if your storyboard has a whole lot of podium sessions in a row, it's not going to be very engaging!"
Choose the room before designing the agenda
Space is engagement infrastructure: no large round tables, natural light, elbow room. When fixed, bend it (knee-to-knee groups; stand-and-turn moves).
Choose the rhythm of rounds
Rhythm/timing is one of the four pattern-language primitives the handbook names.
Compose the question deliberately
Conceptual containers — wicked, appreciative, operational — are one of the four primitives; the question shapes the engagement.
Frame engagement around the five values

Is the engagement designed for Respect (pull-based, sustainable pace)? Courage (upward correction)? Focus on Value (customer satisfaction)? Communication and Collaboration (heard *and* worked-with)? Holistic / Systemic Approach (system, not parts)?
Frame visualisation before the conversation
Is the work visible (board, information radiator) before stakeholders are convened? Conversations on invisible work are conversations on imagined work.
Frame around sustainable pace
Will the engagement (and the work that follows) sustain across cycles? *Muri* degrades engagement quality.
Frame around team-based leadership
Is the team's leadership being engaged, or being bypassed? Open Kanban does not require new titles; it requires team leadership to be present in the engagement.
Decide facilitative-vs-transmissive shape
The foundational shape decision — learning in the conversation (facilitative) vs in the content (transmissive).
Set climate, privacy, and trust posture
Vegas-rules-equivalent, recording-as-consent, content-sharing permission — design choices stated at engagement start, not assumed.
Pick question-form discipline
Open-ended invitational questions for surfacing narrative; closed and directive questions extract specific data but suppress what participants brought.
Decide develop-vs-grade default
Developmental engagement defaults to self-assessment via rubric; grading is explicit opt-in. Default shapes candour.
Find shared purpose with stakeholders
Stakeholders disconnected from the why of the work
Define product/initiative success metric
Stakeholders disagree on what success looks like
Translate strategy to objectives
Strategy not yet translated into team-actionable objectives
Map strategy to behavioural change
Risk of feature-list-driven planning
Capture business model concisely
Stakeholders need a single-page view of the product idea
Imagine the end-state with stakeholders
Quick alignment session needed before detail work
Frame the question to surface mental models, not extract conclusions
The question shapes whether the engagement surfaces or hides mental models. "What do you think is producing this pattern?" surfaces; "Do you agree with this conclusion?" hides.
Test whether the engagement actually warrants systems-mapping ceremony

Apply the broken-ankle test. Some stakeholder situations need a meeting, a calculation, or a quick decision — not a group model building workshop. Don't impose ceremony on situations that don't need it.
Frame an org-change engagement as co-creation vs rollout

"People have to own – not rent – their change." The engagement question is whether stakeholders are being invited to design the change (MAP/ASSESS together) or are receiving a pre-decided design for adoption. Framework-first announcements produce a named set of failure modes.
Choose the vocabulary for cross-discipline org-change engagement

Framework-laden vocabulary (SAFe, LeSS, Team Topologies) pre-loads who is in the in-group. OT's named archetypes are positioned as framework-agnostic discourse that lets specialists from different backgrounds hold "open, unambiguous discussions" — the vocabulary choice is an engagement-framing choice.
Manage cultural attitudes to hierarchy
Stakeholders span cultural boundaries with different hierarchy expectations.
Account for communication-style differences
Direct vs indirect styles or fluency differences may distort engagement.
Adapt to Hofstede's cultural dimensions
Stakeholders span cultures; power-distance, individualism, uncertainty-avoidance, masculinity dimensions shape format and representation choices.
Apply head-body-heart cultural intelligence
Cross-cultural engagement requires more than cognitive understanding; physical (demeanor, eye contact, posture) and emotional/motivational (confidence, motivation) sources matter.
Choose control-oriented vs involvement-oriented engagement
The engagement involves change implementation; top-down monitoring vs participatory goal-setting choice is consequential.
Engage the audit committee in advance
Significant judgements (method changes, estimate revisions, contingencies) are pending; the audit committee's structural role requires forward visibility.
Match topology to good-type
Public goods or common resources require institutional design beyond voluntary cooperation; local self-governance or broader enforcement, depending on context.
Address voiceless stakeholders structurally
Future generations, natural environment, distant communities need credible proxy representation, with consequences for getting it wrong.
Counter information asymmetry in convening design
Engagement quality depends on shared understanding; signalling devices (audits, transparency mandates, third-party certification) needed.
Address concentrated-benefits / dispersed-costs bias
Structural bias toward small organised group versus large unorganised group; mandatory representation or public hearings reduce bias.
Design the customer-engagement format
A customer-engagement event (focus group, co-creation session, advisory board) needs to be designed; the consumer-behaviour framing informs format, timing, and stimulus design.
Convene the legal parties
A negotiation, mediation, or arbitration is being convened; the procedural rules, party preparation, and neutrals' roles need to be specified before convening.
Design for psychological safety
A difficult or high-stakes engagement is being convened; conditions for psychological safety (reduced evaluation apprehension, social-comparison pressure, obedience pressure) need deliberate design.
Choose the elicitation method
Requirements elicitation is being convened; pick interview, focus group, lab study, or exploratory research per stakeholder and question.
Use the think-aloud protocol with users
Users are interacting with a prototype or working software; ask them to verbalise what they're doing, trying to do, asking, and disliking.
Run paper-prototyping swap-and-watch with users
A design needs user feedback before code commitment; hand-drawn screens swapped as the user interacts; cheap and fast.
Run heuristic evaluation across the eight Inclusivity Heuristics
UI design affects users across cognitive styles; multiple evaluators independently check the design against the heuristics via Abi/Pat/Tim.
Run fist of five for team-level commitments
A team is about to commit; six-level voting (None/One/Two/Three/Four/Five) surfaces latent dissent before agreement.
Convene the manager-to-manager conversation around the guide
Two or more managers are discussing whether an individual member of staff requires support or intervention; the guide provides the shared procedural anchor for the discussion.
Couple HR into the engagement
Approaching Q5 (mitigation) or any branch with individual-management response; senior HR engagement must be in parallel — the guide does not replace HR advice.
Run Sprint Review as a working session
The team is about to present results to key stakeholders; collaboration on what to do next is the purpose; Product Backlog may be adjusted in the room.
Adjust the Product Backlog in stakeholder conversations
Stakeholder conversation is producing new opportunities; the Product Backlog is a living artefact and may be adjusted in the Sprint Review.
Surface impediments at the Daily Scrum
The Daily Scrum is the cadence at which Developer-blocking impediments surface; is the conversation focused on Sprint Goal progress and the next day's plan?
Engage the team fully in the Retrospective
The Sprint Retrospective must cover individuals, interactions, processes, tools, *and* Definition of Done — and explore "assumptions that led them astray" and their origins.
Apply Scrum Values in engagement
Is the engagement embodying Commitment, Focus, Openness, Respect, Courage? The values "give direction to the Scrum Team with regard to their work, actions, and behavior."
Give participants control over the convening setting
The setting is the stage; the field where the incident occurred is "almost always" the best location, then the engine bay / saw shop / picnic tables. Avoid "the available conference room."
Use two-interviewer teams for key participants
A key participant or witness is being interviewed; one engages, one notes — with awareness that two can be intimidating, so "with appropriate caution and understanding."
Open with the Ten Principles and Agreements

The group dialogue session is convening; read aloud and agree to the Ten Principles (no admin actions; respectful discussion; learning > blame; vulnerability welcomed; not here to fix; we all make mistakes; almost never careless; rare combinations of normal variability; safety not absolute; safety = reasonableness of risk).
Open the facilitator's "you are not your point of view" frame

Participants are anxious about being judged; open the dialogue with a story where a smart person was convinced and wrong, to lower defensiveness. "Get agreement with your audience upfront that nobody's credibility is on the line."
Position supervisors and Delegating Officials carefully

Supervisors and Delegating Officials should be interviewed (their perspective on risk matters) but should not be present in the group dialogue unless they were directly involved. Strong supervisors suppress voices with different perspectives.
Insulate the FLA from concurrent investigations

OSHA, OIG, claims, prescribed-fire reviews, and law-enforcement investigations proceed alongside but separated. Material evidence is shared; interview transcripts and team deliberations are confidential. Use the Morning Briefing pattern to jointly introduce the investigation teams to interviewees.
Pick the facilitator by experience-in-the-role, not by rank
The TC's rule: when experience and rank are in tension, choose experience. The facilitator must recognise what matters.
Write down the convening agreement
The AAR plan (Figure 2-1 worked example): observer, element, priority tasks, who attends, when held, location, special requirements — all decided in advance.
State the climate rules at the start
The TC's three opening rules — dynamic-candid-professional focused on standards; not a critique; does not grade — are stated, not assumed. Our equivalent must be said out loud.
Design the seating for voice equity

Horseshoe with the engaged-unit leader front-and-centre and higher echelons to the flanks and rear is a deliberate rank-suppression design. Our equivalent: who needs to be physically positioned where?
Treat physical comfort as operational
Helmets off, shelter, refreshments, no facing into the sun, distractions parked away. Comfort is part of the engagement design, not separate from it.
Run the Sprint Review as a 1-hour working session

Seven named segments allocate stakeholder voice deliberately: 5+5+30+5+5+5+5 min covering welcome, highs/lows, demo with critical feedback, Product Backlog state with forecasts, stakeholder market/context updates, next-release planning, collaborative what-next.
Solicit honest and critical feedback at the demo
The 30-minute demo segment standard is "honest and critical feedback" — not validation. Frame the invitation accordingly.
Pre-circulate Backlog Refinement items
The PO sends the item list before the event; invited participants read ahead; the event can end early if items reach Ready. Respects time, encourages brevity.
Use the Parking Lot for stakeholder concerns

A stakeholder concern surfaces at the Daily Scrum that would consume the 15-minute timebox. Defer to the named Parking Lot with voluntary post-event participation; don't lose the concern, don't corrupt the event.
Capture Backlog Items as customer-perspective outcomes
The coffee-temperature contrast: outcomes are stakeholder-language; implementations are developer-language. Backlog Items mixing the two confuse the role boundary.
Offer calibrated forecasts to stakeholders
At Sprint Review: three named forecast formats (scope-by-date likelihood; expected-scope-by-date; expected-date-for-scope). Calibrated, not firmly committed.
Open with a structure that gets everyone speaking early
Impromptu Speed Networking (three rounds); 1-2-4-Whole Group; Knee-to-Knee Conversation set up before arrival.
Use One Minute of Silence before any substantive question
"Everyone benefits from having a moment to think about something before jumping into a discussion."
Replace panels with Celebrity Interviews
"Avoid draining/deadening the energy in the meeting, which happens when people have to listen to any one voice for more than 5 minutes."
Take questions in batches of 3-4
Refuses the Q-A-Q-A pattern that "tends to reinforce the 'central tendency' versus the distributed intelligence of the group."
Honour the ≥20-second wait after questions
Average is 6 seconds; prescription is 20. "If you start talking too soon, you truncate that process and communicate, unwittingly, that you're the one with the answers." Look down at your shoes.
Run Wise Crowds when one person needs option-broadening on a complex challenge
The team's "job is not to decide 'this is what The Client ought to do.'" Client returns with "Here's what I heard that intrigued, surprised, or resonated with me."
Run Troika Consulting for parallel-challenge cohorts
Three-person, 10-min rounds; each is focal once; closing pattern-noticing conversation.
Use Fishbowl when lived experience needs to be centred
Inner circle of 5-6 with direct experience talk to each other, NOT the audience; outer-circle questions; then dialogue between circles.
Use Discovery & Action Dialogues to land in named next-steps
Six-question protocol ending in "What has to happen next? Who will do what when next?"
Apply talking-object discipline in dialogue circles
Especially in Conversation Café and Fishbowl; cultural caveat — respect the talking-stick lineage by naming the borrowing or using a non-religious object (even a rubber duck).
Use Tingsha bells (or a kid's xylophone) to recall participants
Not yelling; not microphone-tapping.
Use the Kanban board as conversation anchor
The board makes work visible to all participants in the engagement — "manager can see status / team member can see impact" levels the asymmetry.
Encourage upward correction
Has the lower-rank-corrects-higher-rank moment happened, or has the engagement stayed top-down? The Courage value names this lever.
Honour pull discipline in the conversation
If a team member resists a commitment, are they being heard (pull) or overridden (push)? Pull-based scheduling applies to engagement decisions, not only work decisions.
Sustain Communication and Collaboration together
Are people being heard (communicated with) and working with others (collaborating)? "One value does not work without the other."
Craft welcome post that sets tone and invites participation
First impression — instructor presence, clear without being too long or too short, ends with participant introduction invitation.
State climate rules out loud
Vegas rules, privacy, recording consent, what-happens-if-people-miss-a-session — stated at start, not assumed.
Set up engagement-equity tracking
Simple spreadsheet — names + blank columns; mark on response; surface inequity over time.
Design accessibility into the engagement up front
UDL multiple-means lens; alternatives to new tools; awareness of how diverse learners perceive the environment.
Pre-decide what to model
Modelling decisions made at design stage — lesson plans (not just slide decks), interactive sessions, feedback-eliciting activities, visible reflection.
Facilitating a participatory mapping session

The facilitator default is to control; the recommended stance is "give away power and responsibility to participants". Diagnostic prompts ("OK, so that means as X increases Y goes down?") clarify rules without patronising.
Capturing and recording stakeholder discussion alongside the map

The discussion is often where the value is, but recording inhibits and transcription is expensive (5–6× recording length). Dedicated note-takers with same-day write-up is the default; photos of the map throughout are non-negotiable.
Designing post-workshop follow-up
Post-workshop energy is a stakeholder-engagement opportunity; one-to-one follow-up calls "can be some of the most useful spaces to develop ideas for analysis and use of maps".
Designing engagement when running multiple workshops sequentially

The full PSM process typically requires multiple workshops; default to mixed groups for the first full version of the map; sequential workshops are an acceptable second-best when mixed groups are infeasible.
Handling stakeholder confusion about the method
Stakeholders misunderstand or misuse the method's rules; explain in two or three different ways; the "explain to a ten-year-old" test; diagnostic-prompt clarification rather than long monologues.
Engaging stakeholders who experience research fatigue

Stakeholders have participated before without seeing value, or are over-engaged across projects; pre-empt by being honest about the value participants will get and designing engagement that gives back, not just extracts.
Structured stakeholder interview
Need to gather user-needs / business-goals / technical-limitations
Capture stakeholder perspective
Designing for a segment whose perspective is not the team's
Map activities and emotions
Need to find emotional pain points and opportunities
Discover business process collaboratively
Business and IT do not yet share a language
Surface jobs the stakeholder hires the product for
Risk of focusing on features rather than outcomes
Equalise voice with senior stakeholders present
HiPPO risk
Make dissent productive
Stakeholders default to deference
Name the unspoken
Conversation skirting a known issue
Establish safety before content
New stakeholder group; first session
Prioritise with stakeholders
Need shared prioritisation decision
Acceptance-criteria conversation with stakeholders
Stakeholder business rules need example-based clarity
Open with Group Model Building when shared mental model matters

When stakeholders need a *shared* picture of the system before they can work on it. GMB (Hovmand 2014) "creates shared insights and common language, and builds consensus for implementing change" — at the cost of "difficult conversations and disagreement", which the design must hold rather than prevent.
Audit facilitator's own positioning

The modeler's archetype-language can "overshadow the lived experience of the participants, who actually understand their situation more deeply." Convene with the facilitator's partial-mental-model named, not concealed under expertise.
Design for amplification, not just inclusion
Participation alone is not enough; the structural design must amplify under-heard voices. Voice and exposure correlate — those least exposed are the most resistant to model revision.
Run the MAP step in the hands-on workplace, not in the back office

The MAP step requires "going to the hands-on workplace to understand how the units work." Convening begins in the place where the work happens, not in a conference room abstracted from it; mapping-in-place is the engagement move, not mapping-then-presenting.
Make the mismatch visible to stakeholders together, not announced to them

When the ASSESS step surfaces a strategic mismatch, "Org Topologies makes it easy for people to see, understand, and then get change going." Co-discovering the mismatch in shared vocabulary is the engagement move; announcing it from authority is the failure mode.
Frame proposed changes as Elevating Katas — named experiments, not directives

"Thoughtful experiments to change the organizational structure, processes, policies." Experiment framing invites participation, accepts uncertainty, and produces learning rather than dictating outcome. Use when a change is being launched into the organisation and you want stakeholder ownership rather than compliance.
Distinguish process from relationship conflict
Disagreement has descended into personal attack; need to reframe to substantive disagreement.
Apply Thomas conflict mode
Need explicit choice of competing, collaborating, compromising, avoiding, or accommodating.
Use cultural intelligence
Same words carry different weights across cultural contexts; head, body, heart needed.
Apply Follett's three conflict paths

Stakeholder conflict has emerged; choice between dominance (short-term resolution), compromise (neither fully gets), integration (creative agreement satisfying both) is consequential for the relationship.
Match power-base to influence path
Reward and coercive power produce alienation; rationality, expertise, and moralistic appeal produce commitment.
Distinguish error from fraud
A misstatement has been identified; honest mistake (correctable through restatement) versus intentional misrepresentation (fraud-triangle case requiring escalation) call for different responses.
Apply the fraud triangle diagnostically
A control failure has surfaced; diagnose which of opportunity, rationalisation, or incentive drove it; control responses target opportunity directly.
Protect whistleblower channels
Internal-audit findings or anonymous reports are surfacing; SOX whistleblower protection applies; suppression compounds the original wrong.
Trace tax / cost / fee incidence
A cost is being assigned to one party; need to know who actually bears it — elasticity determines incidence, not legal target.
Surface externality framing of conflict
One party is asking another to bear an external cost the first does not internalise; Coasean (rights assignment) or regulatory (cap, tax) responses apply.
Test for moral hazard / adverse selection
Conflict is about how risk-sharing has changed behaviour; insurance-design tools (deductibles, mandates, risk-pool design) are the structural toolkit.
Surface ethical conflict using competing theories
Stakeholder conflict is being treated as a power struggle when it has a normative dimension; applying two competing ethical theories (e.g., utilitarian vs. Kantian) names the conflict explicitly.
Apply the TKI negotiation style analysis
A dispute or negotiation has stalled; the five TKI modes (collaborating, compromising, competing, yielding, avoiding) need to be applied to diagnose the impasse.
Surface conformity or obedience as a conflict driver
Conflict is suppressed rather than surfaced; conformity pressure (Asch) or authority-obedience dynamics (Milgram) may be producing false consensus.
Exploit points of departure, not adjudicate them

Accounts of the event disagree; the facilitator's job is to surface the inconsistencies, not to decide which is right. "Diversity of narrative can be seen as an enormous source of resilience in complex system not as weakness."
Walk participants away from re-traumatising recall (deflection questions)

A participant has taken the interview into a difficult point of recall (witnessing a fatality, hearing screaming, etc.); deflection questions on something that went right or that the person knows well mentally walk them away.
Listen for the silent voice
Quiet participants are not contributing; the facilitator must attend to them deliberately. "Don't allow a self-directed dialogue to continue very long unless everyone is participating."
Encourage sensemaking, not adjudication
The group is moving toward deciding who was right; redirect to "achieve a better understanding of the multiple perspectives of the participants." Consensus is not the goal; tension is valuable.
Use open-ended questions, not yes/no
The TC's Bradley-crested-the-hill vs why-didn't-you-engage worked contrast: open-ended invites narrative; yes/no invites self-defence.
Facilitate, don't present
Enter the discussion only when necessary; reinforce that disagreement is permissible; let participants build the picture.
Skilfully steer toward substance
Open-ended questions can produce a soft surface-level discussion; ensure specific issues, positive and negative, are revealed.
Choose the right discussion-organisation technique mid-engagement
Chronological (narrative recall), systemic-BOS (cross-phase patterns), key-events (tight focus when time is limited). Switch when a systemic issue surfaces.
Suppress rank-driven voice silencing actively
If junior voices aren't speaking, the seating, question, climate, or leader-behaviour is wrong. Re-state the no-one-has-all-the-information rule.
Include the OPFOR-equivalent perspective
The TC: "The enemy's perspective is often useful in identifying why a unit was or was not successful." Without it, we have half the story.
Solicit market and context updates at Sprint Review
Named 5-minute segment for stakeholders to share market and context changes during the Sprint. Stakeholders are sensors; capture what's changed.
Surface estimation spread rather than averaging
At Backlog Refinement: a 1-vs-5 estimation spread is information about hidden expertise or hidden risk. Explore it with SMEs/stakeholders present rather than averaging it away.
Run appreciations rounds at the Retrospective

Set-the-stage or wrap-up segment: each team member shares specific appreciation for another. "Alan, I really appreciate how you helped me figure my way out of the memory leak issue…" — engagement quality is repaired in moments like this.
Set the stage with feeling check-ins
Open the Retrospective with each member sharing "one-word or a brief phrase that describes how they're feeling." The check-in lets the team hear each other before diving into substance.
Use the 5-Whys with the surprise signal
When a stakeholder-raised concern needs deepening, ask "why" five times until the root cause emerges. "If you're rarely surprised by your root causes, you may not be digging deep enough."
Refuse default report-outs from breakouts
"'Report Outs'… are BORING!!!" — they drain the energy of small-group conversation. Use Gallery Walk or bring-into-next-conversation.
Engage participants in the output-format decision
"Engage participants in a conversation about who actually needs this material and what might be the best and most useful way for them to get it."
Capture in voice-preserving modes
Graphic recording on long walls; post-it harvests; Take a Panel "stand-alone" sheets; preserve voice rather than facilitator paraphrase.
Sequence the closing as What/So What/Now What
Three questions in order; Now What is informed by the What (data) and So What (pattern) that precede it.
Test for *Muri* (overburden) as a hidden conflict driver
Conflicts that look interpersonal are often *Muri*-driven — exhausted teams produce conflict that respect, sustainable pace, and *Muri* awareness would prevent.
Test for push-vs-pull as a hidden conflict structure
If team members feel "pushed into" work and managers feel "ignored," the conflict is structural (push vs pull), not interpersonal.
Apply the holistic / systemic lens to inter-stage conflict
When one stage of the value chain blames another for delays, "no single part of a system can ever bring overall improvement" — frame the conflict at the system level.
Calibrate presence to phase
Heavy early (community builder, guide); lighter later (coach, mentor stepping back); "less visible" in participant-led phases; "broad and deep" in mid-course feedback.
Model what is being taught
If asking participants to reflect, share own reflection; if asking for lesson plans, use one visibly; if asking for FLIF, complete it.
Resist thoroughness in feedback
In peer-review-centred engagements — comment on one or two aspects, invite participants to elaborate, protect peer-to-peer engagement.
Surface predictable participant experiences proactively
First-week overwhelm; Week-3 anxiety; end-of-course let-down — name structurally rather than letting participants experience them as personal failings.
Notice and respond to Anxious-Annie pattern
When the urge to chase, over-respond, or intervene early is rising — locate it as one's own pattern; trust the process.
Counsel-out a deeply-struggling participant
When a participant cannot meet the design's participation requirements — support a defer-decision rather than allowing degraded participation.
Check engagement-equity tracking
Whose names have few marks next to them? Surface and engage without crowding their voices.
Maintain no-pressuring-tone discipline
Communication that supports engagement, not communication that polices deadlines.
Handling severe disagreement between stakeholders

The disagreement risks derailing the workshop or producing a one-perspective map; the structural responses include separate workshops, preserving multiple views on the map, and avoiding reconciliation as a workshop role.
Handling power dynamics and dominant individuals

The senior or powerful individual dominates the discussion, or even when they don't intend to dominate, others remain silent. The structural moves include positioning yourself as outsider, justifying requests through the method, and (most reliably) separate workshops.
Treat unexpected stakeholder response as a loop, not a personality
A stakeholder's resistance, withdrawal, or sudden flip is usually the visible end of a feedback loop the practitioner hasn't yet mapped. Map the loop before re-framing the stakeholder.
Host difficult conversations rather than smooth them away

GMB "may incite difficult conversations and disagreement" — *this is the work*. Engagement design that protects participants from difficult conversations protects them from what the engagement is supposed to produce.
Use CLDs as engagement structures, not as expert outputs
The CLD is a structured surface on which stakeholders express, negotiate, and refine shared understanding — not the practitioner's analysis to be defended. Iterate on the diagram together.
Diagnose framework-adoption conflict as a disengagement failure mode, not a comprehension problem

When stakeholder resistance, "Religious Wars," or "cargo-cult" compliance surfaces after a framework was adopted top-down, the Primer names this as a psychological phenomenon ("the people didn't figure it out and create it themselves"): more training will not fix it. The engagement redesign is the fix.
Name the target topology's failure mode honestly when surfacing design disagreement

Each OT topology carries a named failure mode (Resource: coordination cost; Delivery: feature bloat; Adaptive: high-variance outcomes). Naming the failure mode honestly when design disagreement surfaces is itself an engagement move that builds trust rather than defending a conclusion.
Apply BATNA analysis
Need to assess whether reaching an agreement is in our interest given alternatives.
Test cross-cultural agreement understanding
Different cultures interpret signed agreements differently (contract-as-end vs contract-as-beginning).
Test against greenwashing
Proposed engagement output is a public statement, sustainability report, or CSR campaign; question is whether substantive practice change accompanies.
Reject ethical relativism defence
Local norms permit conduct (bribery, environmental dumping, sweatshop conditions) the firm's standards prohibit; FCPA / UN Global Compact frameworks apply.
Apply triple-bottom-line accounting
Decision affects people, planet, profit; externalities to non-shareholder stakeholders need explicit weighting.
Determine restatement scope and timing
Material misstatement requires restatement; self-disclosed restatement preserves long-term trust; forced restatement compounds the trust loss.
Frame disclosure for each stakeholder category
A 10-Q / 10-K, press release, and analyst-call combination need consistency; different stakeholder categories read different sources.
Honour consistency through method-change disclosure
A method change is being adopted; full-disclosure-principle application requires explicit explanation in MD&A and footnotes.
Apply Pareto / compensation tests
Need to check whether the proposed agreement leaves some parties worse off and whether compensation is included.
Internalise externalities in the agreement
Agreement may be locally efficient but socially deficient if third-party costs and benefits are absent; tax, charge, permit, or property-rights assignment internalises them.
Address marginal-incentive structure
Compliance behaviour after agreement depends on incentive structure; design for desired behaviour, not for monitoring catch.
Build credible commitment
Without enforcement, reputation, or repeated interaction, agreements unravel under future pressure.
Apply the social-contract test
An agreement is being finalised; the social-contract question (would all parties consent to this behind a veil of ignorance?) is a final-integrity check.
Build the legally enforceable agreement
An agreement is being reached; the contract-formation elements (offer, acceptance, consideration, capacity, legality) and any ADR clause need to be explicit.
Design the customer-value exchange
An agreement with a customer or channel partner is being reached; the value proposition and exchange terms need to be specified at the 4Ps level.
Lock the project priority matrix with the client
A scope-cost-time agreement is being reached with the client; each constraint is tagged Constrain/Enhance/Accept and the matrix becomes the referent for downstream conversations.
Negotiate the Definition of Done per user story
A user story is being committed; what set of acceptance criteria (often in given-when-then format) marks the work DONE-done by the client's standard?
Communicate the recommendation to the affected staff member
The just-culture tree has reached a recommendation; the manager-to-staff conversation needs to describe which branch the case followed and what wider investigation is also under way.
Communicate the differentiation principle to patients and families
A serious patient-safety outcome requires an explanation of why the response to staff involved differs by circumstances; the guide is the structured artefact for that explanation.
Vet recommendations through community-of-practice focus groups (Learning Review)

A Learning Review's recommendations need community-of-practice agreement; multiple focus groups may be needed (line officers, sharp-end crew, technical specialists, supervisors); academic SMEs add an outside perspective.
Validate the story through the readback process

A draft learning product is ready; readback in two phases — those directly involved first, then supervisors and administrators — gives stakeholders the chance to correct and clarify. "Remember this is their story, not yours."
Engage in restorative justice after a serious accident

Employees may be emotionally harmed and relationships strained; restorative justice as the fourth component of Just Culture aims to restore victims to wholeness; the Restorative Justice Checklist is the operational tool.
Agree on what good looks like
"Soldiers must clearly understand what was good, bad, or average about their performance." Has the engagement produced shared understanding of the standard?
Agree on what changes
What SOP, procedure, assignment, training, or relationship change has the engagement surfaced — and has it been locked?
Recognise strengths to sustain alongside weaknesses to improve
The TC's *always strengths to sustain and weaknesses to improve* principle. An engagement that produces only a deficit list creates resentment.
End on a positive note linked to future engagement
The TC's closing-comments rule: review key points, end positively, link to future. Forward-looking framing helps participants leave the room with desire to engage again.
Update the Product Backlog as the ratification of stakeholder voice
Output of the Sprint Review: "An updated *Product Backlog* that reflects stakeholder feedback and new knowledge about the product's context." The artefact ratifies the engagement.
Apply the PO's final-ordering decision rule

At Backlog Refinement: team proposes ordering changes based on value, estimate, dependencies; PO makes the final decision. Team voice informs; PO decides — single point of accountability is preserved.
Capture retrospective improvements as decisions or actions
Decisions = DoD/DoR/Working Agreement changes. Actions = new Backlog Items. Both register commitment in different artefacts.
Communicate the Sprint Goal to stakeholders
The Sprint Goal "communicates why the Sprint is valuable to stakeholders." Has the PO carried it forward in a form stakeholders can hold throughout the Sprint?
Confirm the agreement is sustainable
Will the commitment sustain across cycles, or does it require *Muri*? Sustainable pace is a constraint on agreement quality, not a soft preference.
Make agreements visible
Has the agreement been recorded on the board or other information radiator? Invisible agreements are agreements that drift.
Anchor agreement to value, not to negotiation outcome
The Focus-on-Value value asks: does this agreement create value (or eliminate waste)? An agreement that satisfies the parties but creates no value is not Open-Kanban-aligned.
Design the closing
Synchronous celebration / asynchronous bulletin board / mind map / final forum post — closing is designed, not improvised.
Run structured reflective practice
Learning Journal + weekly nuggets + FLIF-equivalent + Looking-Back/Looking-Forward closing — reflection that is scheduled, structured, modelled, shared.
Harvest the gems
Bring together the collective output of the group and surface it back to the group — making the engagement's work visible to itself.
Schedule co-facilitator debrief
"Don't part company with your co-facilitators without scheduling a debrief" — closure move, not optional.
Anchor org-change agreement on the chain-of-fit (business goal → topology → frameworks as tools)

When stakeholders and leadership agree on a design direction, the agreement should include the chain-of-fit reasoning: which business objective, which org goal, which topology, and which frameworks serve as tools — not the other way around. Agreements anchored only on a named framework have a named failure mode.
Accommodate divergent designs for divergent stakeholder groups

"Companies may also choose different org designs for different parts of the company." When agreement requires forcing one topology onto parts of the organisation that need another (legacy-product service teams vs new-market experiment teams), surface and name the divergence rather than papering over it.
Communicate to non-attending stakeholders
Outcomes need to be communicated to stakeholders not in the room whose acceptance matters.
Plan for political-economy pushback
Agreement creates concentrated losers and dispersed winners; losers will organise.
Build adjustment-assistance
Agreement triggers reallocation (workers displaced, firms exiting, capital redeployed); transition support reduces resistance and addresses distributional concern.
Lock the legal terms
The stakeholder agreement is being ratified; the legal document (contract, consent, MOU, settlement) needs to reflect what was agreed and be executed by parties with authority.
Lock RACI for the work that follows
The engagement has produced agreement; assign Responsible / Accountable / Consulted / Informed before execution starts.
Ratify the parallel wider-investigation engagement

A just-culture decision is being ratified; the wider-investigation obligation is itself a stakeholder engagement (with patients, families, and the broader system) and must be ratified alongside the individual response.
Update the Product Backlog after Sprint Review
Sprint Review produced new opportunities; the Product Backlog has been adjusted accordingly.
Capture Retrospective decisions in commitments
The Sprint Retrospective identified the most impactful improvements; "the most impactful improvements are addressed as soon as possible" — they may be added to the next Sprint Backlog.
Update Definition of Done if quality bar changed
Engagement surfaced quality expectations the team isn't meeting; the Definition of Done is being updated, coordinated with the organisation if a multi-team standard applies.
Translate stakeholder needs into Product Backlog items
New stakeholder needs surfaced; the Product Owner translates them into clearly-communicated, ordered, transparent Product Backlog items.
Publish two distinct learning products (Learning Review)

The Learning Review has produced both an Organizational Learning Product (for policy-makers and senior leaders, focused on the gap between work-as-imagined and work-as-performed) and a Field Learning Product (for district/field employees, designed for dialogue, simulation, or personal reflection).
Ratify Safety Action Plan with named owners and deadlines (Learning Review)
The Learning Review Board has approved recommendations; ratify the Safety Action Plan with named owners and deadlines, consulted before resources are committed.
Disseminate the learning product publicly

Wildland fire-related products are posted on the Wildland Fire Lessons Learned Center; non-fire products through the appropriate channels. Choice of format (written, multimedia, story map, podcast) is the engagement-design decision.
Leave participants space to discuss in private
The convener's exit after closing comments is part of the protocol. Create post-engagement private time for the team to process.
Communicate to non-attending stakeholders deliberately
Attendance scales; not everyone is in the room. What outside-the-room stakeholders need to know, in what form, with what framing?
Revise the convening agreement when the engagement surfaced a problem with it
If the engagement surfaced wrong attendance, wrong duration, wrong format, update the next convening agreement explicitly.
Update the board after the engagement
The Kanban board is the persistent record of stakeholder commitments; updates after engagement carry the decisions forward.
Translate stakeholder asks to backlog items at the right batch size
New stakeholder needs should land in the backlog at a granularity small enough to pull — Open Kanban's batch-size discipline applies to backlog quality.
Capture upward-correction moments explicitly
When courage was used (lower-rank corrected higher-rank), record the learning. Silent absorption forgets the moment.
Close session with action commitments
Session ending; need Who / What / When / With
Record decisions transparently
Stakeholders need decision-history visibility
Ratify the structural intervention, not just the event response

If the engagement surfaced a structural pattern, the commitments must include a structural intervention (loop addition / removal / strengthening / weakening), not just a one-time response to the event.
Communicate that the engagement is an iteration, not the answer

The CLD, the archetype identification, and the agreed actions are all *hypotheses*. Ratify them as such to stakeholders, so the next iteration is expected and welcomed rather than read as failure of this one.
Ratify Elevating Katas as stakeholder-owned experiments with named hypotheses and end-states

The ELEVATE step produces experiments that stakeholders own, not directives they execute. Ratification should name: which Kata, who owns it, what the hypothesis is, and what the exit criterion is. Framing the close this way keeps the engagement-not-rollout posture through implementation.
Close the multi-level engagement loop: confirm all levels are represented in the ratified commitments

"True organizational impact requires alignment across all levels." When ratifying, check whether strategic decisions, HR/Finance policy changes, group/team structural changes, and individual-contributor changes are all named — or whether the commitment is only at one level.
Sustain Mintzberg liaison
Risk that engagement has become reactive rather than maintained; need ongoing investment.
Detect strategic-contingency shift
Hickson's strategic contingencies change over time; stakeholder importance may have shifted.
Maintain the audit committee relationship between cycles
Quarterly emerging issues need forward visibility, not only year-end review.
Sustain regulator engagement
SEC filings, IRS positions, and state-regulator communication need continuity; gaps invite enforcement attention.
Build long-term lender trust through covenant transparency
Debt covenants are under pressure; forward visibility into potential breaches preserves the relationship.
Apply tone-at-the-top in crisis
A control failure or fraud has surfaced publicly; leadership modelling integrity is the leading indicator of recovery.
Monitor incidence drift
Economic incidence shifts over time as elasticities, alternatives, and institutions change; periodic re-check needed.
Track externality changes
Third-party impact may change, with second-order effects emerging that warrant adjustment.
Test for regulatory capture
Regulator was created or empowered; independent review and rotation of personnel correct against capture drift.
Sustain Mintzberg liaison role (management frame)
Stakeholder relationships have become reactive and episodic; sustained liaison-role investment accumulates trust rather than depleting it.
Build the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle
Engagement needs iterative checkpoints rather than one-time execution; PDCA cycle structures continuous improvement of stakeholder relationships.
Monitor CLV and service quality against 5A journey
Customer engagement is ongoing; CLV, NPS, and service-quality metrics (RATER) need to be tracked against the 5A journey benchmark.
Monitor legal compliance post-engagement
A contract, settlement, or regulatory agreement has been entered; ongoing compliance (contract performance, employment law, IP maintenance) needs periodic review.
Track stress and wellbeing in sustained engagements
A long-running stakeholder engagement (change programme, community consultation) carries cumulative stress; GAS cycle monitoring and social-support scaffolding help prevent burnout in participants.
Use Sprint Review for client engagement
An iterative software project is underway; Sprint Reviews are the structured stakeholder-facing demonstration and adaptation moment.
Use Sprint Retrospective for team engagement
The team itself is a stakeholder set requiring sustained engagement; Sprint Retrospectives plan improvements to team quality and effectiveness.
Adjourn the team intentionally
A software project is ending; mark Tuckman's Adjourning stage, recognise contributions, capture lessons learned.
Re-enter the engagement when new investigation facts surface
New facts have surfaced; the guide is re-entrant, and stakeholders (staff member, patients, families) need to be re-engaged when the path through the tree changes.
Maintain the Sprint cadence
Are we holding each Scrum event as prescribed? A dropped Retrospective is a dropped engagement loop. "Failure to operate any events as prescribed results in lost opportunities to inspect and adapt."
Honour Product Owner decisions organisationally
Is the wider organisation honouring Product Owner Product-Backlog decisions, or working around them? "For Product Owners to succeed, the entire organization must respect their decisions."
Continuously refine the Product Backlog
Product Backlog refinement is "an ongoing activity"; is the Product Owner conducting it with the Developers and incorporating stakeholder feedback?
Deliver value between Sprint Reviews
"An Increment may be delivered to stakeholders prior to the end of the Sprint. The Sprint Review should never be considered a gate to releasing value." Is the cadence right for stakeholders?
Remove barriers as Scrum Master
What barriers between stakeholders and the Scrum Team has the Scrum Master surfaced this Sprint? What's being done to remove them?
Make Product-Goal progress visible
Stakeholders want to see the team making progress toward something they understand; is Product-Goal progress being inspected at each Sprint Review?
Sustain restorative-justice engagement after the FLA closes
Relationships and trust do not heal on their own; the next engagement after the next event will suffer if restorative-justice work is treated as one-time.
Sustain liaisons for ongoing employee and family support
The CISM Team, EAP, and family liaisons remain relevant after the FLA's formal close; long-term emotional impact may not surface for months.
Improve the FLA process via team AAR
After the FLA closes, the team itself is a stakeholder set; conduct an AAR of the FLA experience and forward suggestions to `fla.guide.improvements@gmail.com`.
Audit the follow-up
The benefit lives in the follow-up. Has the next-action commitment happened on the timeline committed?
Audit the SOP-equivalent revision
Has the surfaced procedural problem been fixed and used in the next iteration?
Audit the relationship
The TC's claim: AARs promote unit bonding and esprit. Has the engagement strengthened or weakened the relationship between the parties?
Apprentice the next facilitator
The TC: inexperienced AAR leaders observe properly conducted AARs before leading. Are we coaching the next facilitator by example?
Hold the trust-building goal alongside feedback
The Sprint Review names trust-building as a co-equal goal alongside Product Backlog feedback. Trust is built over many Sprints; don't treat it as overhead.
Bring snacks and create informal time
Bring snacks (even remote); the "connect and celebrate" segment after the Retrospective is named informal-engagement time. Small interventions with disproportionate effect.
Don't cancel the Daily Scrum on the last day of the Sprint
The last day is where the team coordinates the final effort against the Sprint Goal. Engagement doesn't take a half-day off.
Watch for status-meeting drift mid-event
If the Sprint Review is degrading into a presentation, or the Daily Scrum into a report-to-PO, name it and reset the frame. Both events have explicit anti-status contrarian positions.
Maintain the confidentiality rule across retros
Vegas Rule or Chatham House Rule chosen at one retrospective should persist as a feature of the team's engagement contract with itself. Revisit deliberately; don't quietly drop.
Watch for "we skipped the retro" drift
Retros being skipped, shortened below 1½ hours, or perfunctory mean the team is losing its primary engagement-with-self mechanism. Surface and recover.
Engage stakeholders daily, not just at Sprint Review
Agile principle #4 framing: the Scrum Team should work daily with business people. If contact is concentrated at one event per Sprint, deepen the daily cadence.
Watch for the half-life problem
"The half-life of the energy and commitment to new ways of being after these events can be short." What structural changes did the engagement produce, not just motivational ones?
Apply 15% Solutions across implementers
Aggregate distributed agency without waiting for the 85% (broader context) to change first.
Run Appreciative Interviews on the engagement's after-life
"What we focus on tends to get bigger." Surface working patterns before debugging broken ones.
Audit the Levels of Accountability ladder
"What do we need to do to work 'above the line?'" Engagement that drifts below stops producing action.
Continue DADs over time
DADs work best as short conversations sustained over time, not as one-off interventions.
Run a What/So What/Now What as the closing
Three sequential questions in order; refuse the temptation to jump to Now-What.
Use Take 10 (Minutes) as a closing reflection
"How has your thinking changed? What have you heard that's really got your juices flowing?"
Use a Conversation Café Final Round
"Each person says briefly what challenged, touched or inspired them"; talking-object-mediated.
Wave hands instead of clapping for in-meeting acknowledgement
Preserves mood; small craft of the closing moments.
Maintain the engagement cadence
Open Kanban does not prescribe a cadence but names Retrospectives, Strategy Meetings, Kaizen Groups as forms — pick one and hold to it.
Sustain Movement / field-level engagement
If the team is engaged in a community of practice, is participation actually happening — pull requests, conference talks, contributions back?
Watch for engagement going one-way
Is engagement still bidirectional, or has it slid into one-way reporting, one-way command, or one-way feedback?
Close the engagement cycle with a learning step
"Learning is the key concept before continuous improvement can ever happen!" — did the engagement cycle close with a Retrospective, Strategy Meeting, or Kaizen Group?
Respond to self-assessments where perception differs
If participant's self-assessment and our perception differ — reach out directly to invite discussion.
Read course feedback and reflect on facilitation
Reflection while fresh — make notes of things to enhance or change for next iteration.
Continue connection where appropriate
Keep email addresses, engage past participants in a community of practice — extending engagement value beyond formal close.
Surface and integrate standing-list of predictable participant experiences
What predictable participant experiences in this engagement type should we name proactively in future iterations?
Retrospect with stakeholders
End of project/sprint; need stakeholder reflection
Refresh stakeholder map
Organisational changes since last engagement
Visualise work for stakeholders
Stakeholders need at-a-glance progress view
Surface cross-silo pain
Multiple silos working on related problems independently
Build inter-team agreement
Cross-team friction or dependencies
Launch a new group
New team-of-teams forming
Multi-party relational contract
Transactional contract insufficient for ecosystem collaboration
Watch for archetype repetition across engagements

If successive engagements with the same stakeholder group keep producing the same shape of disappointment or unmet expectation, the pattern may be a recurring archetype — *Drifting Goals* (commitments quietly lowered), *Shifting the Burden* (the underlying conditions go unaddressed), *Limits to Growth* (the engagement runs out of capacity).
Treat the stakeholder relationship as a stock under continuous flow
The relationship's state is an accumulation; each engagement adds (or drains) trust, attention, alignment. Single events don't carry the work; continuous flow does.
Re-surface mental models periodically

Mental models drift; what was shared after the engagement may have diverged since. Schedule re-surfacing, especially with stakeholders insulated from the system's current pressure (whose models are stickiest).
Use periodic re-mapping as the recurring stakeholder-engagement rhythm

"Periodic re-mapping with Org Topologies is an easy and fast way to complete a learning loop of feedback and adapting. We are never finished with the perfection journey." Schedule re-mapping with the same stakeholders engaged in the original MAP session to close the engagement loop rather than treating the elevation as a one-shot event.
Watch for drift back to Resource-Topology framing and low-voice patterns

If the language of "resources," "headcount allocation," or "skill-demand matching" is creeping back into how people are described, that signals Resource-Topology drift — the engagement consequence is predictably declining ownership and voice. Name it explicitly.
